doc.file_path: /mnt/data/Potkay et al. - 2021 - Coupled whole-tree optimality and xylem hydraulics explain dynamic biomass partitioning.pdf

extraction.coverage_note: C001 covers Notes S1 intro and S.1 (partial): allometric scaling & structural metrics up to Eqs. S.1.3 (inclusive).

model.expanded_name: Tree Hydraulics and Optimal Resource Partitioning
model.high_level_goal: Predict dynamic biomass allocation by coupling whole-tree hydraulics with an optimality framework (details of objective function referenced but not fully defined in this chunk).

simulation_config.extension_rule: If growth is slow due to resource limitation, extend simulation until achieving similar total tree dry structural biomass as end of control simulation (~106 g).

forcing_data.source: NCEP/NCAR Reanalysis 1 (NOAA re-analysis; Kalnay et al., 1996)

components_in_scope.component: Model overview + run configuration (Notes S1 intro)

referenced_components_out_of_scope.note: Mentioned in intro; not extracted in C001.
Mentioned in intro; not extracted in C001.
Mentioned in intro; not extracted in C001.

referenced_components_out_of_scope.component: Respiration/senescence (S.7), allocation (S.8), carbon pool dynamics (S.9), simulation descriptions (S.10)
referenced_components_out_of_scope.note: Mentioned in intro; not extracted in C001.

state_representation.notes: Belowground geometry not explicitly modeled beyond carbon pools per soil layer.

assumptions_and_omissions.assumption: Model uses a single canopy layer (one leaf carbon pool C_L) but multiple soil/root layers (C_{R,i}).
Belowground organs are not given an explicit physical representation (e.g., root length/area), beyond root carbon pools per soil layer which characterize uptake potential.
Empirical form factor ξ and stem carbon density ρ_{cS} are assumed constant in wood carbon-volume relation.
Wood volume relations implicitly assume area-preserving branching (da Vinci's rule) and uniform sapwood area along the flow path (pipe model).
Delayed xylem refilling/permanent xylem damage not represented; immediate/idealized refilling implied.
Nutrients assumed non-limiting; nutrient uptake and partitioning not explicitly simulated.
Osmoregulation / detailed NSC metabolism and phloem transport not mechanistically represented in this version; discussed as future module.

equations.label: (S.1.1)
equations.description: Wood structural carbon pool related to stem volume via empirical form factor and carbon density.
Heartwood carbon-volume relation implied by same form as Eq. S.1.1 using transition diameter.

equations.label: (S.1.2)
equations.description: Tree height scaling as a power-law function of basal diameter.

equations.label: (S.1.3)
equations.description: Canopy width scaling as a power-law function of basal diameter.
Total canopy leaf area equals leaf carbon pool times specific leaf area.
Total wood carbon is the sum of sapwood and heartwood pools.
Total root carbon is the sum over soil layers and their horizontal/vertical root pools.

variables.name: leaf carbon pool
variables.notes: Single canopy layer in this implementation.

variables.name: total root carbon pool (integrated over soil layers)
root carbon pool in soil layer i
variables.notes: Decomposed into horizontal and vertical pools.

variables.name: horizontal root carbon pool in soil layer i
variables.notes: Represents absorbing (fine) roots + local xylem pathway.

variables.name: vertical root carbon pool in soil layer i
variables.notes: Represents transporting roots (xylem), interpreted as a taproot.

variables.name: basal diameter
variables.notes: Used in scaling of H, W, and wood volume relation.

variables.name: leaf water potential
variables.notes: Referenced in discussion of Gain-Risk algorithm and delayed refilling; defined later (S.3/S.6).

variables.name: leaf allocation fraction
variables.notes: Referenced as allocation fraction; formal definition in allocation section (S.8).

variables.name: sapwood allocation fraction
variables.notes: Referenced; formal definition in allocation section (S.8).

variables.name: horizontal root allocation fraction
variables.notes: Referenced; formal definition in allocation section (S.8).

variables.name: vertical root allocation fraction
variables.notes: Referenced; formal definition in allocation section (S.8).

variables.name: whole-tree growth rate
variables.notes: Referenced in discussion of NSC/phloem-growth module; formal definition later (S.9).

evidence.snippet:
19 
 
Notes S1 – Full model description and simulations 
FULL MODEL DESCRIPTION 
 This appendix describes the THORP (Tree Hydraulics and Optimal Resource Partitioning) model.

Symbols and units are summarized in Table S1, and the order of model computations are summarized as a 
flowchart in Figure S12. In this study, the control simulation was run for a total of 100 years at 6-hour 
time-steps (Δt = 21600 s)

based on the resolution of NOAA re-analysis data that was used as atmospheric 
boundary conditions (NCEP/NCAR Reanalysis 1; Kalnay et al., 1996), which are air and soil 
temperatures, Ta and Tsoil [K], respectively, relative humidity of the air, RH [-], precipitation rates, P [m∙s-
1], windspeeds at 10 m elevation, u10 [m∙s-1], and incoming solar insolation, Is [W ∙ m-2].

We note that THORP represents key plant mechanisms but ignores others which may serve as 
secondary drivers of allocation response. Among those, four notable mechanisms are (1) delayed xylem 
refilling or lack of recovery due to permanent xylem damage, (2) plant water storage, (3) nutrient uptake 
and partitioning, and (4) osmoregulation

20 
 
Additionally, performing Sperry et al.’s (2017) Gain-Risk algorithm for a delayed-refilling case 
would require solving the algorithm twice (once to solve for the optimal leaf water potential, ΨL, 
using the virgin vulnerability curve, and a second time in search of the stomatal conductance that 
coincides with the same ΨL, except using the nonvirgin vulnerability curve

(2) THORP ignores plant water storage, since our representation of xylem hydraulics is largely based 
on Sperry et al. (1998; which included hydraulic capacitance, but it was set to zero), and since no 
previous tests of the Gain-Risk algorithm have included plant water storage

(3) THORP currently assumes that nutrients are neither limiting nor alter the optimal path for 
allocation (which is the common framework for many past optimality models; e.g. Franklin, 
2007; Mäkelä et al., 2008; McMurtrie et al., 2008). Consequently, we do not explicitly simulate 
nutrient uptake and partitioning.

(4) A mechanistic representation of osmoregulation (and its links to nonstructural carbohydrate 
(NSC) metabolism and phloem transport) would have no effect on THORP’s predictions of the 
allocation fractions (uL, usW, uR,H, uR,V) at least for the same physiological and environmental 
conditions.

The simulated tree is represented by two aboveground carbon pools: leaf and wood carbon pools, CL 
and CW [mol C], respectively. The latter is the sum of a hydraulically-conductive and respiring sapwood 
pool, CsW [mol C], and nonconductive and non-respiring heartwood pool, ChW [mol C] (CW = CsW + ChW).

22 
 
layers. The root carbon pool of an individual soil layer is denoted by CR,i [mol C], where the subscript i 
signifies the index of the soil layer. Each soil layer contains two distinct root pools: (1) a pool responsible 
for conducting water within the soil layer ‘horizontally’, CR,H,I, and (2) a pool responsible for conducting 
water among the soil layers ‘vertically’ towards the aboveground portion of the tree, CR,V,I.
It is notable that the model includes multiple root and soil layers (i.e. multiple CR,i), but not  canopy layers (i.e. single CL).

The relevant tree dimensions are total canopy leaf area, aL [m2], height, H [m], canopy width, W 
[m], basal diameter, D [m], and the diameter of the heartwood-sapwood transition, Dt [m]. Leaf area is the 
product of the leaf carbon pool and the specific leaf area (SLA), ΧL [m2 ∙ (mol C)-1] (aL = ΧL ∙CL).

ΧL [m2 ∙ (mol C)-1] (aL = ΧL ∙CL). Wood 
carbon-volume relations in THORP are similar to those used by Buckley and Roberts (2006a,b) and 
Mäkelä (1986).
